user_review:
I'm done reading climate fiction unless it's written by Richard Powers.

Alt review: it's a thin line between a good book and just a fine book (derogatory).

Double alt review: I am no hater of the Poor Communication Kills trope, but like, come on fam

title: Vigil
user_review:
With recent Saunders (this and Liberation Day) I found myself craving more of the intense novelty that permeates his earlier collections. This book shares the same conceit as Lincoln in the Bardo, which is a great conceit for exploring themes on morality and humanity, but loses some of the freshness of reading about a limbo-world for the first time.

That being said, this book still delivers all the good Saunders-stuff that has him as my favorite contemporary author. He portrays his characters with a blunt sincerity that truly understands them without judgement.

Recency bias, but I found myself thinking about Joachim Trier and how his movies are filled with real, three-dimensional people instead of archetypes of good and bad. Same with Saunders (except sometimes the real people are ghosts

title: The Correspondent
user_review: Easy reading, creative structure, well-wrought main character, funny at points, sad at points, what is not to like here??

title: Slouching Towards Bethlehem
user_review: Candidly, I struggle with collections of essays (which is surprising because I love collections of short stories). Not doing myself any favors by reading this late at night, I would sometimes catch my eyes just scanning the words. However, for the essays that caught me, and there were quite a few, I was loving the prose and structure. In particular, "On Keeping a Notebook" and "Goodbye to All That" resonated deeply and personally (having kept a journal for years, and having had a dreamlike 6 months of living in nyc in my early twenties).

title: Harry Potter and the Sorcerer's Stone (Harry Potter, #1)
user_review:
Full-cast Dolby audiobook for commuting / solo workouts.

This was a really fun new addition to the many many many ways to consume the Harry Potter world. Shall I list them all? The original books, the Jim Dale audiobooks, the Stephen Fry audiobooks, the movies, dramione smut, the old PC video games, the newer Switch video game, the future HBO series.

And now this, a full-cast audiobook with mind-boggling 3D sound effects and great voice acting (some of whom you will recognize, Matthew Macfadyen so good!)

title: Pale Fire
user_review:
Wow this was l i t e r a r y, and it was just at the outer limit of my attention span outside of an academic setting (don't get many of those these days do I). I was only able to get through thanks to the long break from work and work related stress. I'm glad I had the chance because in the end this was a very rewarding puzzle to unpack and enjoy.

At some points I wished to have this in print format for easier flipping back and forth, but I appreciated Kindle's X-Ray feature for the first time ever, and I very much relied on its dictionary (was looking up about one word per page).

With all the cross-references and intertextual links, by the time I finished I felt like I'd read the entire book three times.

title: Barbarians at the Gate: The Fall of RJR Nabisco
user_review: Just like Succession except it’s a 600 page book and it all actually happened

title: The Secret History

Ngl the bacchanal sounded pretty lit. I'm going to call up my Great Books TA from Michigan and lodge a complain.

3.5 / 5 stars: fun to read but got a bit repetitive, draggy and aimless in the back half.

title: Kafka on the Shore
user_review: This was a really strange book... good thing I like strange books!

title: The Great Gatsby
user_review:
Audio book for the new commute. Banger after banger of quotable lines that have been seared in my head since high school.

Favorite deeper cut line from this re-read re-listen:
"What'll we do with ourselves this afternoon," cried Daisy, "and the day after that, and the next thirty years?"

title: The Last Life
user_review: Smooth reading, but the lack of a driving narrative will make this forgettable for me, in the end

title: The Elements of Investing
user_review: Funemployment is as good a time as ever to remember the fundamentals!

title: Frankenstein
user_review:
Missed this classic in High School (shame on you Shaker!). It was absolutely nothing like I expected it would be based off pop culture. I was waiting for mobs, Fritz/Igor, and "it's alive" before realizing all that stuff was added by later movie adaptations.

Instead I got stories with in stories told in beautiful language and varying structure on themes of ambition, science, love, etc etc etc. Anyway, it was great.

Shoutout to Guillermo del Toro for the catalyst to read.

title: James
user_review: I didn't really vibe with the writing style - too much telling and not enough showing. There were some bits in there I did enjoy (the reclaimed code switching, small philosophies on god, huckleberry ret cons) but in the end this was a book I was reading just to get to the end so I could read a different book.

title: Sunrise on the Reaping (The Hunger Games, #0.5)
user_review:
This is an example of a prequel that actually causes a character to lose depth. Haymitch was a fully three-dimensional character in the original trilogy, with a backstory and that was shown, not told, and understood through dialog and choices.

This prequel was a cameo-jammed, overly retconned, unnecessary retelling of a story that packed a bigger punch in less than ten pages of Catching Fire.

The only saving grace - even though I complained about it a sentence ago - is that each cameo did make me squeal "omg I KNOW them."

title: Station Eleven
user_review:
Re-reading this post-pandy hits a lot harder. The chapters that describe the fallout from the flu went from being interesting sci-fi to fridge-horror what-ifs about the real pandemic.

Such a solid work of literary-ish sci-fi. I could see someone criticizing this book for having an anti-climatic ending, but they would have missed the the fact that it was all about the journey.

ORIGINAL 2017 REVIEW BELOW

I'll always love a well executed, deeply intertwined, mosaic piece.

Read this very shortly after reading The Road, and the astounding differences in tone were fun to think about.

title: The Seven Moons of Maali Almeida
user_review:
This was a creative, kafkaesque take on the bardo, and naturally it reminded me of my favorite George Saunder's novel, but MAN I did not connect with the writing style. The second person threw me, I found it tonally confused, and my god the acronyms just wouldn't stop.

On the plus side I learned a lot about Sri Lanka and TAMIL came up in a nyt crossword while I was reading it so there's that I guess.

title: Playground
user_review:
Absolutely wonderful. I liked this significantly better than The Overstory and I already liked that one a lot. It took a little to get going, but once the characters and story were established it started to hum. Like his other books, this has themes of environmentalism, technology, friendship, love, and family. For a lot of other novels that would feel like too much, but Powers is so good at weaving themes and meanings into the narrative that it never felt like this was overreaching.

I am a big fan of meta-review about "cinematic moments" I had sitting in movies theaters and "literary moments" I had reading something wherever I was reading it. Well, this gave me a big one as I stayed up to finish it alone in a ski lease in Tahoe. I am putting it up there with a few other notable moments like:
- Finishing 100 Years on a boat with my family
- Reading book 7 of HP for the first time curled up in my childhood bedroom
- The end of And the Mountains Echoed on a ferry to Martha's Vineyard

title: Onyx Storm (The Empyrean, #3)
user_review:
I think if I tried to list out all my complaints they would be as long as the book itself. But I will try to list a few!
- so many fucking names
- absolutely ZERO character development
- bizarre pacing, esp during scenes that should have been intense, everything just felt... flat
- filler plot that accomplishes just about nothing
- infuriatingly repetitive conversations between main characters

The only reason this gets 2 stars and not 1 is the hangover effect from the first book. I still like the world that was built and am desperate for more crumbs of information about it.

Where were you when the era of dragon smut died?

user_review:
Yes that's right I started 2025 off by reading smutting HP fanfic

This was OK, it had a good premise but started dragging in pace about halfway through and never recovered

title: Klara and the Sun
user_review: Engaging read, because I absolutely love Ishiguro's style, but not as moving or memorable as Never Let Me Go or Remains of the Day. It felt a bit emotionally flatter and, the surrounding world was less realized.

title: Machines like Me
user_review: There were some interesting ideas tossed around, but in the end I found it a bit unfocussed and annoying. Maybe all the talk of P=NP was just too close to home for me... I had flashbacks to my introductory Computer Science theory course at umich

title: Defending Jacob
user_review: Fun crime slash court room thriller

title: North Woods
user_review:
Here is a little list of books that, if you liked them a lot, I think you will like this book a lot:
· The Overstory
· Lincoln in the Bardo
· Cloud Cuckoo Land

Those three books have all been my favorite in the year that I read them so naturally, I loved this book. It is a sweeping mosaic novel that is grand in scope and varied in structure. I loved almost all of the sub-stories (with one notable exception that I didn't vibe with). In the end I found that all the connective tissue of the story really held together for a cohesive whole. 10/10 will be re reading one day.

title: Recursion
user_review:
Candy Sci-Fi Beach Read (same category as Dark Matter)

Super loose spoilers below for the first 1/3 of the book.

The writing is passable, and serves as a fine platter to serve up some heady, mind-bending time travel ideas. You know it's a good one when you're out there diagramming timelines on the back of a napkin to explain things.

Definitely got some Eternal Sunshine vibes from the beginning.

For Dark Matter, I said I wanted to see a movie adaptation directed by Denis Villeneuve or Ryan Johnson. For this one, I think Shane Carruth is the only director that could handle the complexities.

title: The Overstory
user_review:
This has a lot going for it
- enormous, resounding scope
- trees
- structural complexity
- more trees
- thematic complexity
- did I mention the trees?

I felt a little far away from the book at points, that honestly might have been because I was not in a situation where I could give it full attention. This is a book that needs your full attention.

Here's to starting 2024 with a harder one, gonna go read a bunch of easy ones to buff the stats

title: The Remains of the Day
user_review:
I really love Ishiguro's prose. It wanders around and around, but manages to stay completely focussed. Every digression either forwards the plot or adds depth and nuance to a character. He manages to slowly reveal new plot points at an extremely measured pace through flashbacks and memories. I remember feeling the same way reading Never Let Me Go.

This book was full of interesting musings on memory, goodness, dignity, human connection, and love. Now I'll go watch the movie.

title: Dark Matter
user_review:
(beach read)^2

candy sci fi

I would rather have watched this as a movie adaptation directed by Denis Villeneuve or Ryan Johnson

title: Four Thousand Weeks: Time Management for Mortals
user_review:
"Hmm what a good point, we do all rush through life" I thought to myself as I rushed to finish this book, check it off my list, and get back to work /s

I approached this with hesitancy, because I hate pop psych self help books, but it was actually good! Some parts were freshman-smoking-weed in a dorm philosophy like "we are time" and some parts were more targeted thoughts on time management. There were enough interesting points in it that kept me reading to the end

title: Trust
user_review:
This took a while to really catch me, but man it was the read. The threads of the novel are perfectly woven together for a really satisfying conclusion. Themes of music and money and memory and storytelling are blended to profound effect.

I love books within books and stories within stories and this novel had a lot of that. The writing was fantastic and I learned a lot of new words along the way.

title: Tomorrow, and Tomorrow, and Tomorrow
user_review:
Super fun beach read page turner! Made me think a lot about my game design capstone project back at Michigan. This was a very smooth read, and I really enjoyed some of the structural creativities throughout (writing from the perspective of a video game, second person present for a chapter, etc etc etc). Some of the dialogue felt cheesy, but other parts felt really authentic and insightful.

My only true complaint is that it felt a little thesaurus-y at parts, with random SAT words smooshed in there. That being said, I am sure that same complaint could be leveled at me... so I am not trying to throw too many stones here.

In the morning, from bed, I reached for my kindle instead of my phone to keep reading, which has not happened in a really long time.

title: Beneath a Scarlet Sky
user_review:
Nice historical fiction! Definitely fun to read this on a honeymoon to northern Italy. Was a bit disappointed after doing a little research in the real Pino Lella, but hey it’s historical fiction right

Read this with Sarah and everyday we would discuss and the main point was “shit we really know nothing about WWII history.” That’s on us for both skipping APUSH in high school

title: This Is How You Lose the Time War
user_review: Somehow both predictable and unpredictable at the same time. By that I mean that the overall arc was a little basic and unsurprising, but the structure, details, and language continued to delight me in creative ways. In the end it was quick, fun, and I have thought about it a bit since reading, so I recommend for anyone who wants a little dose of heady, time-travel sci fi!

title: Liberation Day
user_review:
George Saunders just doesn't miss. Excellent collection of stories.

Personal favorite was My House, but all of them were creative, profound, insane, funny, etc etc etc. You know, the usual Saunders stuff.

Some stories contained echoes of his earlier works (Liberation Day -> The Semplica-Girl Diaries and Ghoul -> Pastoralia), but all managed to inject enough new-ness that they didn't feel derivative at all.

title: Tender Is the Flesh
user_review:
I was fooled by a tik tok book influencer into reading this, and so I am to blame for having to suffer through it. The premise was pretty dark, but that is about the only good thing this book has going for it.

Not sure if it was bad writing or bad translation or both, but it was nearly impossible to palate. At some points I actually put on my tin foil hat and wondered if I was reading a book written by a computer...

title: Cloud Cuckoo Land
user_review: Astounding in scope, this mosaic-novel is essentially a love letter to the power of stories. It traces five seemingly unrelated story arcs across hundreds of years, but as you expect the narrative threads delicately start to weave into each other in very subtle and thought provoking ways. The novel blends historical fiction with sci-fi with contemporary fiction but manages to be completely cohesive. I have a lot more to say about this, but I will leave it out of the review. Just go read it.

title: Someone Who Will Love You in All Your Damaged Glory
The Magician's Land (The Magicians, #3)
user_review: Just a bit less engaging than the seconds book (which itself was just a bit less engaging than the first book). Worth a read just to get some closure on the trilogy's built world, but much less memorable.

title: The Magician King (The Magicians, #2)
user_review:
Not as good as the first book, but still good. In my review of the first one I called it a cross between Harry Potter and Narnia. To follow that template, I would call this one a mix between Narnia and Lord of the Rings. We follow the heroes on a quest to attain 7 keys, while exploring more deeply the fantasy world that was set up in the first book.

This book also gives a deeper backstory for a character from the first installment in a series of alternating chapters with the main plot. I found myself more captivated by the secondary plot, and was reminded of the second Eragon book where I was more engrossed in the Roran Stronghammer subplot than the main storyline.

Worth a read if you liked The Magicians a lot, but that being said, it was definitely a noticeable notch down in terms of writing quality.

title: The Magicians (The Magicians, #1)
user_review:
Imagine if Harry Potter and the Chronicles of Narnia had a child, but that child was depressed, mopey, and a little horny; maybe that child would look something like this book. Replete with lampshade-hangy references to to former parent and suspiciously devoid of those to the latter, The Magicians sits somewhere in between the two works.

It starts by exploring some college-age students in a magic university and transitions into an intense fantasy world quest novel, all while playing with adult themes like drugs and sex and big questions like "what if all your dreams came true but you were still unhappy?"

I love this book and have read it a few times. That being said, I can understand not liking this book if you don't like stories with characters that are hard to like (at least at first). I am re-reading now so I can remember it all before reading the third book, which I read once, but can't remember much about.

title: Pride and Prejudice
user_review:
The prose took some getting used to - which is to be expected considering it was written over two hundred years ago - but once I warmed up to the style, I was quite surprised to find this book something of a page turner. There I was, up at one in the morning to find out what would happen next between Elizabeth and her suitor... never would have guessed.

I liked the antiquated words and the use of second definitions (which were probably first definitions back then). I loved the long, winding sentences that only revealed their meaning in the ultimate or penultimate clause, and found myself often needing to re-read a paragraph to fully decode it.

title: The Andromeda Strain
user_review: Fast-paced action sci-fi at its finest. The story is very contained: 5 main characters, 5 days, 1 underground lab. Lots of hard science fiction. The book is structured as a write-up that occurred after the events of the novel, which leads to good foreshadowing and clues. Very quick read.

title: Triangulum
user_review:
I did not like this book at all. The characters felt flat and hard to connect with, the writing was not so good. Since this was sci-fi, all of that would have been ok if the plot was gripping, but it wasn't. I thought it was all over the place and convoluted.

Sorry Nolan

title: The Dinner
user_review:
Very fast read.

Most manipulative narrator I have read since Lolita...

All in all the story is gripping, but the characters are so sociopathic that I found it hard to immerse fully.

title: Maybe You Should Talk to Someone: A Therapist, Her Therapist, and Our Lives Revealed
user_review: Interesting read for me because, you know, my mom's a shrink.

title: CivilWarLand in Bad Decline
user_review:
Loved it!

Much rougher, rawer, and darker than his later short stories.

The personal afterthoughts were fascinating, to learn more about his context when writing this first collection of stories.

title: The Mythical Man-Month: Essays on Software Engineering
user_review: Pretty out-of-date, but that is what I get for reading a book about software engineering that was written two decades before I was born...

title: A Swim in a Pond in the Rain
user_review:
I'll never be lucky enough to take a course with George Saunders as the professor, but this book might be the next best thing.

Across 7 short stories, Saunders delivers pedagogical essays and personal afterthoughts that touch on details like form, pattern and pacing, as well as larger ideas like interiory of monologue and contextual meaning (to name only a few).

Too soon to say, but I hope (and believe) that this book has strengthened the "active reading" part of me that is constantly questioning the author's choices from all the way "across the pond".

I'll end with a quote I loved from the prologue: "To study the way we read is to study the way the mind works: the way it evaluates a statement for truth, the way it behaves in relation to another mind (i.e., the writer’s) across space and time."

title: Harry Potter and the Methods of Rationality
user_review:
This was more fun than it had any right to be. Harry Potter meets Enders Game meets Artemis Fowl.

Really came into its own stride with the first Dementor encounter.

Is this what Methadone feels like?

title: To Be a Man
user_review:
Fantastic collection of short stories.

Personal favorites: Seeing Ershadi & The Husband

title: Leave the World Behind
user_review:
Quasi-literary suspense, pretty fun.

I thought the writing in the first third was noticeably better then the rest, but by that point I was fully engaged in the plot, so ¯\_(ツ)_/¯

title: Circe
user_review: This was a FUN read. I knew much of the underlying myth and yet the retelling in Circe was unwaveringly entertaining and gripping. Highly recommend for anyone, regardless of familiarity with the Greek epics at the kernel of the book.

title: Less (Arthur Less, #1)
user_review:
This book follows the peripatetic Arthur Less on his journey around the wold. It's filled with fun musings on love, middle age ennui, and many scattered Ulysses/Odysseus references.

The style is light and cinematic. Really fun read

title: Masterpieces: The Best Science Fiction of the Twentieth Century
In Persuasion Nation
user_review:
I like reading earlier stuff by Saunders bc it's cool to see him experimenting with batshit insane stories, threshing out the unique voice that he will eventually refines for collections like 10th of December and books like Lincoln in the Bardo.

That being said, some of the short stories in this one were a little over the top for me...

Loved "I CAN SPEAK!" and "Christmas" broke my heart

title: Here, There and Everywhere: My Life Recording the Music of the Beatles
user_review:
Great look at the Beatles time from a "fly on the wall" perspective.

Lost some energy near the end when he focusses on their solo work

title: Exhalation
user_review:
This is fantastic literary sci fi.

Chiang masterfully blends hard science fiction tropes like time travel and parallel universes with lofty philosophical ideas like fatalism, free will, and the power of language. Some stories in the collection are so good they are worth a read and then an immediate re read.

title: Pachinko
user_review: A generational tale with themes of identify, home, and stoicism. Highly recommend

title: The Restaurant at the End of the Universe (The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy, #2)
The Hitchhiker’s Guide to the Galaxy (Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy, #1)
Buried in the Sky: The Extraordinary Story of the Sherpa Climbers on K2's Deadliest Day
Touching the Void: The True Story of One Man's Miraculous Survival
user_review: Not as theatrical or grandiose as Into Thin Air, but just as harrowing, if not more so. Be warned, descriptions of the Joe's leg will make you wince.

title: Colorless Tsukuru Tazaki and His Years of Pilgrimage
Alice’s Adventures in Wonderland / Through the Looking-Glass
Exit West
user_review:
Brilliant and fairytale-like, this story is filled with insightful gems and poetic musings on emigration, religious, relationships, and the beauty of life.

This was one of those books I finished, and had the immediate desire to start again from the beginning.

title: Little Fires Everywhere
user_review:
Lots of drama packed into such a small book. While it was engaging and fun, this book won't stick with me. Would be a great beach/candy read though.

The intense Shaker setting was at best nostalgic (she calls out my favorite mural!) and at worst intrusive (not used to my hometown being peered at from an 'outsider' for hundreds of pages).

title: 4 3 2 1
user_review:
This book is engaging and brilliant. A man lives four lives. He grows, the arcs diverge, and meaning can be found within each subplot, as well as in the differences between them. Sometimes he befriends the same people, sometimes he doesn't.

At a young age, all humans play the if game: "But what if I had--? Or maybe if I--? What would my life be if I just--?" This book explores that to the max through 900+ pages answering questions on destiny, fatalism, soulmates, and life.

title: Children of the Mind (Ender's Saga, #4)
user_review:
Upon re-reading this, I had the immediate desire to have kept it shrouded in the nostalgic glow of my favorite childhood sci fi saga. Now I'll always lump it into the "mediocre" pile.

Also, I missed a lot of intense Mormon culture glorification when I read this years ago, and that wasn't exactly my cup of tea.

Heady and brilliant, this book is "neither fiction nor nonfiction, but a flickering between them."

The New Yorker story, The Golden Vanity, is the kernel of this work, and if you like the story, then you will like the book.

title: Leaving the Atocha Station
user_review:
The main character is anxious, drugged out, a little pretentious, and kind of an asshole... And I still identified with him and enjoyed reading his neurotic thoughts throughout this book.

Not as good as 10:04, but if you liked that you would probably like this.

title: Nutshell
user_review:
Creative, funny, and replete with satisfying callouts to a different work (won't spoil the fun by naming it). This book was a pleasure to read!

It was the first book by Ian McEwan I've read, but it won't be the last.

title: The Goldfinch
user_review:
I can't remember the last time I gave up on a book...

The Goldfinch did not engage me in any way - not the language, not the plot, not the characters. I want my two weeks of reading back.

title: The Rock Warrior's Way: Mental Training for Climbers
The Underground Railroad
user_review: A great read, but my favorite thing about the book is still the premise.

title: Into Thin Air: A Personal Account of the Mt. Everest Disaster
The Elegance of the Hedgehog
user_review: Too pretentious for me, and I am already pretty pretentious...

title: The Road
user_review:
Grim, horrifying, hopeless...

For best results, read alone in a semi-lit room.

title: The Old Man and the Sea
The Light Fantastic (Discworld, #2; Rincewind, #2)
user_review: Not as fun and wonderful as The Colour of Magic, but still a good fix of Discworld antics. Worth a read if you enjoyed following Rincewind and Twoflowers in book 1.

title: The Color of Magic (Discworld, #1; Rincewind, #1)
user_review: Intensely fantastical, charmingly absurd, and downright fun

title: Harry Potter and the Cursed Child: Parts One and Two (Harry Potter, #8)
The Brief Wondrous Life of Oscar Wao
